per_question.question: Why was the fastapi-cli command line tool introduced?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #11522 (merged) added the `fastapi` command (FastAPI CLI) to give a simpler getting-started and dev/run experience.
per_question.answer:
The fastapi‑cli command line tool was introduced to add a new `fastapi` command to the FastAPI distribution [PR #11522].  
The PR titled “✨ Add FastAPI CLI, the new `fastapi` command” explicitly added this command‑line interface [PR #11522].  
During the PR discussion a maintainer noted that the feature would help FastAPI newcomers the most by providing a friendlier getting‑started experience [PR #11522 discussion].  
The tool was merged as part of that effort, despite concerns about the merge process [PR #11522 discussion].  
No other purpose or functionality is stated in the provided context.

Discussion on PR #11522: ✨ Add FastAPI CLI, the new `fastapi` command

bnbong (2025-03-01): Hello @tiangolo !

I'm a Korean student who loves FastAPI very much, and I've been using and studying FastAPI for years.

I am writing to introduce you to my project "[FastAPI-fastkit](https://pypi.org/project/FastAPI-fastkit/)," which I recently released as a dedicated supporter and developer within the FastAPI community.

## Project Background

I've been following the community's response to the `fastapi-cli` package introduced in FastAPI version 0.111.0. While some users expressed concerns about functionality of `fastapi-cli`, I was deeply inspired by your intention to lower the entry barrier for newcomers to the FastAPI framework.

Motivated by your philosophy, I developed a project that aims to provide a richer experience for FastAPI beginners while also being valuable for experienced developers.

## Introducing FastAPI-fastkit

FastAPI-fastkit is a comprehensive CLI tool inspired by the strengths of SpringBoot Initializer and django-admin CLI. It's designed to allow users to quickly generate and extend structured FastAPI projects with simple commands.

GitHub: https://github.com/bnbong/FastAPI-fastkit

## Key Features

- Creation of structured FastAPI projects with simple CLI commands
- Consistent project structure through modularized boilerplates
- Easy expandation : ex) attaching a new router endpoint at FastAPI project via cli
- Time reduce for configuring FastAPI projects

I started this project to contribute to your goal of "lowering the entry barrier to FastAPI" that you aimed to achieve with fastapi-cli, and I recently released version 1.0.0.

`FastAPI-fastkit` was just released today, it doesn't have that many features yet. However, the functions currently implemented have been developed with a focus on improving a lot of what I think is a barrier to entry for FastAPI based on the experiences of FastAPI around me and the response of the domestic community in my home country, Korea.

I would appreciate your thoughts or advice on this project that aims to benefit the FastAPI community. I would also be delighted to discuss how this project could further contribute to the FastAPI ecosystem.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Best regards,

tiangolo (2024-08-02): Thanks for the feedback @Jaza and everyone!

As having `fastapi` include by default the standard dependencies has been inconvenient to several people, I decided to make those standard dependencies an optional extra group `standard`.

So, to get the standard optional dependencies people now have to install it with:

```bash
pip install "fastapi[standard]"
```

**Note**: remember to use the quotes, depending on your terminal that might be required.

Having the optional extra, square brackets, quotes, is not as nice as just `fastapi`, it's extra things to have in mind, and extra things for newcomers to learn from the beginning. But anyway, it seems that having the default standard dependencies installed by default, and having to change the installed package to be `fastapi-slim` when people didn't want those standard dependencies instead was being too inconvenient for some people so I decided to switch back to have `fastapi` as the minimal/slim version and the standard extra dependencies in the group `standard`.

This is available in FastAPI version 0.112.0. :tada:

Jaza (2024-07-07): @tiangolo a few comments:

- I'm a FastAPI fan, I've been using it for the main codebases that I work on at my full-time job for about 2 years, no plans for that to change anytime soon
- I know there has been criticism of the way the FastAPI project is run, but personally I don't have a problem as such with your ongoing BDFL role, it's your baby, you can keep holding all the keys to the castle if you want, the quality of FastAPI has in my opinion benefitted greatly from your oversight
- You can of course give priority to whatever you want, you can build whatever features you want, that's what open source is all about, if you have an itch to scratch then go for it

However:

- FastAPI is now quite a mature project with hundreds of contributors
- This is a fairly significant new feature
- You merged this PR less than an hour after you opened it
- Nobody had time to review it or to give any feedback whatsoever
- AFAICT there was no issue thread to discuss this, nor was there prior discussion anywhere else
- No mention of a CLI in the [roadmap](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/10370), or anywhere else that I'm aware of, AFAICT nobody had any heads-up about this
- Many of the comments, since this PR was merged, have been critical - some are critical of the feature itself, others are critical of the rationale and the process (or lack thereof) behind it

Personally I think it's a decent feature (and, on par with FastAPI's high standards, it appears to be thoroughly tested and clearly documented). It will probably help out FastAPI newcomers the most, with a friendlier getting-started experience. I don't need it urgently, not sure if I'll use it, I might give it a try. Anyway, what the feature is that you built, isn't the point here.

In future, could you at least leave a PR (for a non-critical new feature) open for a reasonable time, before merging it? I would suggest one week, to give people some time to review and comment.

And ideally, could you advise the community in advance, in a separate issue thread, and/or in the roadmap, of features that you plan to work on?

I'm not suggesting a radical overhaul of how the project is managed. I just think that a little bit more transparency, and a little bit more engagement, are not unreasonable expectations of the community, for a project that thousands of people now rely on. And I don't want to see people abandon FastAPI on account of these concerns (as some of the comments in this PR have suggested, and as I've seen suggested in a number of discussions elsewhere), I think it's a great project, and I'm saying all this in the hope that it helps rather than harms the project's future.

per_question.question: What bug did app.include_router have with nested lifespans and how was it fixed?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #9630 (merged) ensured include_router merges nested lifespans so routers' lifespan contexts run instead of being dropped.
per_question.answer: The bug was that `app.include_router` did not execute or merge router lifespans, so nested lifespans were ignored [PR #9630]. The PR fixes this by modifying `include_router` to merge the original and nested lifespan contexts and yield their combined state, ensuring nested lifespans run correctly [PR #9630].

PR #9630: 🐛 Ensure that `app.include_router` merges nested lifespans
Author: Lancetnik | created 2023-06-06 | merged
Labels: bug, p1

I am working on my own [Propan](https://github.com/Lancetnik/Propan) library and as a part of it I implemented a custom **FastAPI** router with the following behavior.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from propan.fastapi import RabbitRouter

router = RabbitRouter("amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672")
app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)
```

To connect RabbitMQ at **FastAPI** startup I used `router.on_startup` events and all worked fine, but now it is deprecated. So I migrate to `lifespan` and now **FastAPI** doesn't run router lifespan at the code above.

I suppose it was missed cuz the original `include_router` method includes all nested `on_startup` and `on_shutdown` deprecated events [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L1307), but doesn't includes a router lifespan.

So, the following code doesn't work

```python
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter

@asynccontextmanager
async def router_lifespan(app):
    print('router start')
    yield
    print('router shutdown')

router = APIRouter(lifespan=router_lifespan)
app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)
```

The current PR fixes the original framework miss.
All original test and lint.sh checks works fine.

per_question.question: What changes were needed to support Python 3.14?
per_question.ground_truth: PRs #14110 and #14165 (both merged) added Python 3.14 support: CI test matrix entries and dependency/metadata updates.
per_question.answer:
PR #14110 added support for Python 3.14 [PR #14110].  
PR #14165 also added support for Python 3.14 [PR #14165].  
PR #15149 extended the test suite with a configuration for `python-version: "3.14t"` [PR #15149].  
The same PR allowed the test suite to run when `orjson` and/or `ujson` is not installed [PR #15149].  
It removed `orjson` and `ujson` from standard test dependencies [PR #15149].  
It added an additional test configuration to cover deprecated `ujson`/`orjson` tests [PR #15149].  
It ensured that `fastar` 0.9.0 is used [PR #15149].  
It fixed `orjson` and `ujson` issues on free‑threaded runs and fixed SQLAlchemy cyextension issues on Ubuntu [PR #15149].  
These changes were implemented in commit 4b26487 [commit 4b26487].  
Support for Python 3.14 was initially added in commit 3611c3f [commit 3611c3f].

PR #15149: ⬆️ Support free-threaded Python 3.14t
Author: svlandeg | created 2026-03-18 | merged
Labels: upgrade
Linked issues: #13946

Continues from #13946: adding support for free-threaded Python 3.14t

* Extend test suite with a configuration for `python-version: "3.14t"`
* Allow the test suite to run when `orjson` and/or `ujson` is not installed, cf also https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14964 which deprecated `ORJSONResponse` & `UJSONResponse`
* Remove `orjson` & `ujson` as a standard test dependency
* Add an additional test configuration to test for deprecated `ujson`/`orjson` tests (to ensure coverage goes back to 100%)
* Ensure that `fastar` 0.9.0 is used

## Checklist

- [x] Fix issues with `orjson` & `ujson` on free-threaded run
- [x] Run `orjson` & `ujson` tests in one specific non-free-threaded run
- [x] Fix `fastar` issue on free-threaded run: https://github.com/DoctorJohn/fastar/releases/tag/v0.9.0
- [x] Fix SQLAlchemy cyextension issues on Ubuntu

## Open question

Decided not to do this:
- [ ] Bump `pydantic-core` & `strawberry-graphql` (and maybe others) to allow running `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` on 3.14t as well. This will negatively impact other users on older Python versions / older external lib versions.

per_question.question: How does FastAPI support free-threaded Python 3.14t?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #15149 (merged) added support and CI configuration for the free-threaded 3.14t build, adjusting optional dependencies that lacked free-threaded wheels.
per_question.answer:
FastAPI added a test configuration for `python-version: "3.14t"` to run on free‑threaded Python 3.14 [PR #15149].  
The PR extends the test suite with that configuration [PR #15149].  
It allows the test suite to run when `orjson` and/or `ujson` is not installed and removes them as standard test dependencies [PR #15149].  
It ensures that `fastar` 0.9.0 is used for the free‑threaded run [PR #15149].  
It also adds an additional test configuration to test deprecated `ujson`/`orjson` tests to restore full coverage [PR #15149].  
The discussion in PR #13946 notes that `3.13t` is skipped because many libraries, such as `pydantic-core`, do not support it [PR #13946].  
It mentions that `orjson` lacks free‑threaded support and that support is expected only in a later version [PR #13946].  
The support was merged in commit 4b26487 which explicitly adds free‑threaded Python 3.14t support [commit 4b26487].

ngoldbaum (2026-03-18): It looks like they need an update either to declare support for the free-threaded build or simply update to the latest PyO3 version, which does that automatically. I might poke at that today to help you out here.

svlandeg (2026-03-18): > It looks like they need an update either to declare support for the free-threaded build or simply update to the latest PyO3 version, which does that automatically. I might poke at that today to help you out here.

That would be awesome, thank you so much!

ngoldbaum (2026-03-18): I opened https://github.com/DoctorJohn/fastar/pull/60.

ngoldbaum (2026-03-23): Looks like the Mac and Windows tests aren't picking up fastar 0.9.0.

svlandeg (2026-03-23): > Looks like the Mac and Windows tests aren't picking up fastar 0.9.0.

It's unclear to me why the `3.14t` test on Windows wasn't installing the latest `fastar` 0.9.0, https://github.com/fastapilabs/fastapi-cloud-cli has it at `"fastar >= 0.8.0"`. Anyway for now I explicitely require 0.9.0 and then that test does succeed 🎉 

The Mac test is a different issue: I was running that one with `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` which means that it tries out the lower bounds of our supported ranges. Here, it crashed for an older version of Pydantic, which is not surprising. Now the only open question is whether we want to bump those lower bounds (which may be annoying for users on older versions of Python & external repos), or just leave them and assume that users running `3.14t` will also have updated dependencies 🤷

svlandeg (2026-03-23): Ok cool, confirmed that bumping to `pydantic >= 2.12.0` and `strawberry-graphql >= 0.284.0` allows the test suite to run succesfully with `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` on Python 3.14t.

Discussed this internally with Sebastián and decided that we don't want to go so far as bumping Pydantic to 2.12+, as it might create issues for users still on older versions. So, I'll remove this test configuration again and then this PR should be ready for review.

- [ ] Bump `pydantic-core` & `strawberry-graphql` (and maybe others) to allow running `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` on 3.14t as well. This will negatively impact other users on older Python versions / older external lib versions.

Discussion on PR #13946: ✅ Run tests on free threaded python 3.14

svlandeg (2025-09-24): Had a look into this.

I suggest to skip `3.13t` which was in beta and not supported by several libraries anyway. For instance, `trio` only supports `3.14t` (and up), and `pydantic-core` also [removed testing](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-core/pull/1738) for `3.13t` .

To be able to run our test suite on `3.14t` in the CI, as far as I can see, we'd need 

- [x] `httptools` to release wheels built against Cython 3.1+ (I verified that it installs fine [locally](https://github.com/MagicStack/httptools/issues/118#issuecomment-3165444672))
- [x] `pydantic` 2.12.0 to be released with 314t wheels
- [x] `bcrypt` <s>to release [3.14 support](https://github.com/pyca/bcrypt/pull/1073)</s> -> 5.0.0 is now released with free-threaded Python 3.14 support
- [ ] `orjson` to start supporting free threading (some relevant discussion [here](https://github.com/ijl/orjson/issues/591)) 

I'll open a PR to start testing fastapi on (normal) Python 3.14 so we can work on that separately: <s>https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14110</s> https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14165

CC @ngoldbaum: as I've seen many contributions from you on various libraries on this topic, does the above ☝️  sound about right? Any other feedback or pointers is appreciated 🙏

ngoldbaum (2025-09-24): > does the above ☝️ sound about right? Any other feedback or pointers is appreciated 🙏

`httptools` isn't a blocker for me personally so I haven't touched it yet. I think it should be straightforward to update if anyone wants to take that on. It'd be really nice to add free-threaded wheel builds to their build automation.

Both pydantic and bcrypt should have releases with 3.14t support soon, I think.

orjson is trickier, I don't think there will be free-threaded support before 3.15. I know that pydatic-core uses https://github.com/pydantic/jiter rather than orjson. I'm not sure how tightly coupled fastapi is to orjson.

per_question.question: What was proposed to improve jsonable_encoder performance, and did it merge?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #13982 proposed refactoring jsonable_encoder with a performance bump; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer: The performance boost was to be achieved by refactoring `jsonable_encoder` with dict/list comprehensions and reduced set operations, which the author reported gave about a 14% speedup [PR #11256]. That PR was marked closed unmerged, indicating it was not merged into the codebase [PR #11256]. A later attempt to refactor `jsonable_encoder` for performance (PR #13982) also remained unmerged and was still under discussion [PR #13982].
contexts:
PR #11256: Performance improvements for jsonable_encoder
Author: Zaczero | created 2024-03-07 | closed unmerged
Labels: refactor, p4

Hello! This is my first contribution to FastAPI so don't hesitate to point out any issues so I can improve. Today I have focused on improving the performance of **jsonable_encoder** as it seems to be a hot path in many applications.

Given this [test file](https://openaedmap.org/api/v1/countries/PL.geojson), there seems to be about ~14% performance gain. Most of which come from dict/list comprehensions and reduced set operations.

**Before**
```sh
python -m timeit -n 15 -s 'from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder' -s 'from pathlib import Path' -s 'import json' -s 'data = json.loads(Path("PL.geojson").read_bytes())' 'jsonable_encoder(data)'
```
> 15 loops, best of 5: 145 msec per loop

**After**
```sh
python -m timeit -n 15 -s 'from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder' -s 'from pathlib import Path' -s 'import json' -s 'data = json.loads(Path("PL.geojson").read_bytes())' 'jsonable_encoder(data)'
```
> 15 loops, best of 5: 125 msec per loop

Obviously, your experience may vary depending on the environment and the data. Having said that, I believe those are safe and widely applicable optimizations.

Feel free to ask questions - I am very active on GitHub and I tend to respond quickly.

Discussion on PR #13982: ⚡️ Refactor `jsonable_encoder` with performance bump

**Breaking Changes:** - Pydantic v1 compatibility removed - Documentation deleted - Type hints changed to older style **Public API:** - jsonable_encoder performance improved (positive) - Pydantic v1 support removed (breaking) **Test Coverage:** - No information on test changes (diff too large to analyze) **User Impact:** - **Positive:** Performance improvements for jsonable_encoder - **Negative:** Breaking changes for Pydantic v1 users - **Negative:** Loss of documentation - **Unclear:** Migration path for Pydantic v1 users ## Assessment **Comment Only** This PR contains valuable performance work but is too large and combines multiple breaking changes. ### Recommendations **Split into Multiple PRs:** #### PR 1: Performance Refactor (Merge First) **Scope:** ONLY jsonable_encoder performance changes - Refactor encode_value, encode_dict functions - Add NoneType constant - Update ENCODERS_BY_TYPE - Include benchmarks - **NO breaking changes** - **NO documentation deletions** #### PR 2: Remove Pydantic v1 Compatibility (Major Version Bump) **Scope:** ONLY Pydantic v1 removal - Remove _compat directory - Update error handling - Add deprecation warnings first, then remove in next major version - Update version to 1.0.0 (or next major) - Include migration guide - Update documentation #### PR 3: Documentation Cleanup **Scope:** ONLY documentation changes - Review and update deleted docs - Ensure critical documentation is preserved - Add new docs for changes in PR 1 and PR 2 ### Specific Concerns 1. **No Migration Guide:** Pydantic v1 users need clear guidance on how to upgrade 2. **Version Bump:** Removing Pydantic v1 compatibility requires a major version bump 3. **Documentation Loss:** Ensure no critical information is lost in doc cleanup 4. **Reviewability:** Current scope makes thorough review impossible The performance work is valuable and should be merged. The Pydantic v1 removal is also likely necessary, but should be a separate, clearly communicated major version change. **Recommendation:** Split this PR into at least 2-3 separate, reviewable PRs. Merge the performance refactor first, then handle breaking changes in a major version update.

per_question.question: What functionality was missing from ValidationException, and what happened to the proposal to add it?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #12972 proposed adding the missing ValidationException.__str__; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer: The missing functionality was a human‑readable string representation (`__str__`) for `ValidationException` that would output validation errors in plain text [PR #12972]. The proposal to add this method was submitted in PR #12972, but the PR has remained open unmerged for over a year and the author noted reluctance to invest further effort without any indication of acceptance [PR #12972].

gtadamson (2023-08-10): @tiangolo,

We currently have that recommendation (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/extending-openapi/#self-hosting-javascript-and-css-for-docs) implemented, but with a recent release of FastAPI, the tool is re-writing the version of the openapi spec that we provide to a higher version upon deployment. The static files that are saved are incompatible with the new version of the spec and all of our swagger pages are broken when we upgrade.

One solution that you might propose is to upgrade all the static files for every server every time FastAPI changes the openapi spec version. This is what we're trying to avoid. The solution above allows us to have a single point to change the files. I think it also keeps our repositories cleaner (no copy of static files that are unrelated to the domain problem we're trying to solve) and simpler for more junior developers by abstracting these details into a single location.

I respectfully ask if you'll reconsider your decision.

Thank you for your time and effort, we have migrated to FastAPI for all of our new servers.

per_question.question: Was Scalar integrated as an alternative documentation UI for FastAPI?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #10674 proposed adding Scalar integration as an alternative to Swagger UI/ReDoc; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
Scalar was proposed as a third built‑in alternative API documentation UI for FastAPI [PR #10674].  
The proposal added a `scalar_url` parameter, a `get_scalar_html()` helper, and automatic route mounting when both `openapi_url` and `scalar_url` are set [PR #10674].  
The PR was marked as “closed unmerged”, indicating that the integration was not merged into the main FastAPI codebase [PR #10674].

nidhishgajjar (2026-04-15): **Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM 5.1 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev)) ## Summary This PR adds [Scalar](https://github.com/scalar/scalar) as a third built-in alternative API documentation UI alongside Swagger UI and ReDoc. It introduces a `scalar_url` parameter on `FastAPI` (defaulting to `/scalar`), a new `get_scalar_html()` helper in `fastapi/openapi/docs.py`, and updates documentation, examples, and tests. ## Architecture The implementation closely mirrors the existing patterns for Swagger UI and ReDoc, which is the right approach: - New `scalar_url` parameter on the `FastAPI` class constructor, same shape as `docs_url` and `redoc_url` - New `get_scalar_html()` function in `fastapi/openapi/docs.py` that generates the HTML response - Auto-mounted route in `FastAPI.__init__` when both `openapi_url` and `scalar_url` are set - Custom docs UI examples updated to demonstrate self-hosting - Tests follow the same structure as existing Swagger/Redoc tests This fits cleanly into the existing codebase architecture. ## Issues ### 1. Empty image file — `docs/en/docs/img/index/index-08-scalar-02.png` (Warning) **File:** `docs/en/docs/img/index/index-08-scalar-02.png` The diff shows this file was added with 0 bytes (`index 000000000..e69de29bb`). The file is empty — it looks like a placeholder was committed instead of the actual screenshot. The README and features docs reference this image, so it will appear as a broken image in the documentation. **Fix:** Replace with the actual Scalar screenshot image. ### 2. Unpinned CDN URL for Scalar JavaScript (Suggestion) **File:** `fastapi/openapi/docs.py` The default `scalar_js_url` is set to `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@scalar/api-reference`, which resolves to the **latest** version. In contrast, the existing CDN URLs use version-pinned paths: - Swagger UI: `swagger-ui-dist@5` - ReDoc: `redoc@2` An unpinned URL means a future breaking release of Scalar could break existing FastAPI applications without warning. **Fix:** Pin to a specific major version, e.g. `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@scalar/api-reference@1`. This can be updated as new major versions are released. ### 3. HTML interpolation without escaping (Observation — pre-existing pattern) **File:** `fastapi/openapi/docs.py`, `get_scalar_html()` The `title`, `openapi_url`, and other parameters are interpolated directly into the HTML via f-string without HTML escaping. This follows the exact same pattern used by `get_swagger_ui_html()` and `get_redoc_html()`, so it's consistent. However, if `title` contained `<script>` tags or similar, it would be injected into the response. This is a pre-existing concern across all three docs UI functions, not something introduced by this PR. ## Cross-file Impact - **`fastapi/__init__.py`**: Not modified, and correctly so — `get_scalar_html` is imported directly from `fastapi.openapi.docs` in example code, matching how `get_redoc_html` and `get_swagger_ui_html` are used. - **`.github/DISCUSSION_TEMPLATE/questions.yml`**: Adds a checkbox for Scalar, which is a nice touch. - **`docs/en/docs/how-to/custom-docs-ui-assets.md`**: Mentions `scalar.standalone.js`

per_question.question: What happened to the proposal to support context managers directly as dependencies?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #10353 proposed supporting context managers as dependencies; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
The proposal to let context managers be used directly as FastAPI dependencies was implemented in PR #10353 [PR #10353].  
That PR was closed unmerged and never merged into the main branch [PR #10353].  
Community members repeatedly asked for the feature to be merged, but no merge occurred [PR #10353].  
Later, issue #11143 reported that context managers in Depends were broken after version 0.106, showing the proposal remained unimplemented [Issue #11143].
contexts:
Discussion on PR #10353: ✨ Support using context managers as a dependency

nidhishgajjar (2026-04-14): **Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM 5.1 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Review of PR #10353: Support using context managers as a dependency

This is a great feature addition — allowing context managers to be used directly as dependencies is a natural ergonomics improvement that eliminates boilerplate wrapper functions. The implementation approach is sound and the test coverage is solid.

### Bug in documentation example 🔴

In `docs_src/dependencies/tutorial010_ctx_usage.py`, the `get_db()` function is decorated with `@asynccontextmanager`, but the usage example uses `with` instead of `async with`:

```python
# tutorial010_ctx.py defines:
@asynccontextmanager
async def get_db():
    ...

# tutorial010_ctx_usage.py uses:
async def migrate():
    with get_db() as db:  # ← Bug: should be `async with`
        ...
```

This will raise `RuntimeError: no context manager` at runtime. It should be:
```python
async def migrate():
    async with get_db() as db:
        ...
```

Or alternatively, change `tutorial010_ctx.py` to use `@contextmanager` (sync) if you want to demonstrate a sync context manager usage.

### Implementation observations 🟡

1. **Scope of change**: The PR modifies the `else` branch in `solve_dependencies` (sync, non-generator, non-coroutine callables). This correctly handles sync functions that return context managers. However, async non-generator functions that return context managers (e.g., `async def get_db(): return SomeAsyncCM()`) are not handled — they go through the `is_coroutine_callable` branch and the returned CM is not entered. This is a known limitation and can be addressed in a follow-up, but it's worth documenting.

2. **`contextmanager_in_threadpool` compatibility**: The sync context manager path wraps the result with `contextmanager_in_threadpool()`. This works for generator-based context managers (from `@contextmanager`), but may not work correctly for class-based context managers (with `__enter__`/`__exit__` methods) since `contextmanager_in_threadpool` expects a generator/iterator interface. This is an edge case but worth noting.

3. **Check ordering**: Checking `__aenter__` before `__enter__` is correct — async context managers should take priority.

### What looks good ✅

- **Clean implementation**: The three-way check (async CM → sync CM → plain value) is straightforward
- **Proper exit stack integration**: Using `async_exit_stack.enter_async_context()` ensures cleanup happens correctly
- **Good test coverage**: Tests for both sync and async context managers, verifying both setup and teardown
- **Documentation restructure**: The reorganized docs flow better, introducing the simple case first
- **Technical Details note**: Explaining the internal `__enter__`/`__aenter__` check is helpful

### Summary

Solid feature with good tests and documentation. The one blocking issue is the `with` vs `async with` bug in the documentation example, which would confuse users trying to follow the tutorial.

**Assessment: request-changes** (doc example bug needs fixing)

PR #10353: ✨ Support using context managers as a dependency
Author: notpushkin | created 2023-09-29 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature, p3, conflicts

## Background

Currently, you can't pass a context manager to `Depends()`, as it would treat it as a function. This means that if you want to use a function both as a dependency in FastAPI and a context manager (e. g. in scripts or Alembic migrations etc), you need to define both versions:

```py
# For FastAPI:
async def get_session():
    async with AsyncSession(engine) as session:
        yield session

# For scripts:
get_session_mgr = asynccontextmanager(get_session)
```

---

Unfortunately, as @a-recknagel pointed out in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/8955#discussioncomment-5666195, it's impossible to check whether given function is a context manager (defined with a decorator from contextlib) or not:

```py
@asynccontextmanager
async def get_session():
    yield 42

get_session.__aenter__  # => AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute '__aenter__'
```

It is however possible to check that after we call the function:

```py
called = get_session()  # => <contextlib._AsyncGeneratorContextManager at ...>

if hasattr(called, "__aenter__"):  # => True
    solved = await stack.enter_async_context(called)
else:
    solved = await called
```

This PR implements that check (for async context managers only for now – sync version would require doing some threadpool magic I'm still looking into :-)


# Related discussions
* https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/11122
* https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/8955
* https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/9054

If depends only closed after the response has completed, you could use them to turn the context into a procedure that returns the iterable and is cleaned up after the request has completed.

uriyyo (2025-10-05): For those still waiting for the official FastAPI fix - you can use `fastapi-backports` until the official release.

```bash
pip install fastapi-backports
```

Brings multiple Query models and PEP 695 type alias support to `FastAPI` until it is officially supported:

https://github.com/uriyyo/fastapi-backports

Usage example:
```py
import fastapi_backports.apply  # noqa: F401

from typing import Annotated, Any

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class UserFilters(BaseModel):
    min_age: int | None = None
    max_age: int | None = None


class GroupFilters(BaseModel):
    min_size: int | None = None
    max_size: int | None = None


type UserFiltersDependency = Annotated[
    UserFilters,
    Query(),
]

type GroupFiltersDependency = Annotated[
    GroupFilters,
    Query(),
]


@app.get("/filters")
async def get_filters(
    user_filters: UserFiltersDependency,
    group_filters: GroupFiltersDependency,
) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return {
        "user_filters": user_filters,
        "group_filters": group_filters,
    }
```
```py
GET /filters?min_age=10&max_size=20

{
  "user_filters": {
    "min_age": 10,
    "max_age": null
  },
  "group_filters": {
    "min_size": null,
    "max_size": 20
  }
}
```

per_question.question: Was an option to ignore trailing slashes in routes added to the FastAPI class?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #12145 proposed an ignore-trailing-slashes option on the FastAPI class; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
Yes, an option to ignore trailing slashes was added to the FastAPI class [PR #12145].  
The option is a boolean flag named `ignore_trailing_slash` that defaults to False and can be set when creating the FastAPI instance [PR #12145].
contexts:
PR #12145: ✨ Add ignore trailing slashes option to `FastAPI` class
Author: Synrom | created 2024-09-06 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature
Linked issues: #7298, #9328

Add a boolean flag `ignore_trailing_slash` to FastAPI class that decides whether to ignore trailing slashes at the end of URIs.

For example, by setting `ignore_trailing_slash` to True, requests to `/auth` and `/auth/` will have the same behaviour.
By default (`ignore_trailing_slash` is False), the two requests are treated differently and one of them will result in a 307-redirect.

Using the flag is as easy as:
```python
app = FastAPI(ignore_trailing_slash=True)
```

This change provides a simple solution to discussions #9328 and #7298

Discussion on PR #12145: ✨ Add ignore trailing slashes option to `FastAPI` class

Option* in FastAPI CLI to allow the forwarded headers from the proxy. I just finished/tweaked a PR that added the `--forwarded-allow-ips` CLI Option and released a new version of `fastapi-cli` to include it (version 0.0.10). ### Solution: Second Step Then, the other part, and probably the most important one, was the documentation for how all this works and how to properly set up the application server behind a proxy, so I wrote a whole new section here with all the details to handle these cases, how the proxies work, the headers involved, how to set it up, etc.: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/behind-a-proxy/#proxy-forwarded-headers There's even a nice diagram explaining the details. :nerd_face: ### Current alternative to this solution Now, let's say that you read all my reasons and you don't agree for some reason, and you just want to have the behavior of always serving the requests with or without a slash without redirects. You can achieve it with the same middleware from this PR, adding it to your code: ```Python from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send class _IgnoreTrailingWhitespaceMiddleware: def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp): self.app = app async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None: if scope["type"] in {"http", "websocket"}: scope["path"] = scope["path"].rstrip("/") await self.app(scope, receive, send) app = FastAPI() app.add_middleware(_IgnoreTrailingWhitespaceMiddleware) api_users_router = APIRouter(prefix="/api/users") @api_users_router.get("") async def users_index(): return {"status": "success"} app.include_router(api_users_router) ``` ### Future alternative solution I have been planning for a while to make a refactor in how routers work, in a way that should allow people to customize and use the routers. This way it would be possible to create a custom router that handles this logic and can be shared by the code base of that specific application, even if the feature is not in FastAPI directly. But for now, I think that if you still want that feature, the custom middleware would work, and would do pretty much what this PR is currently doing. --- Given all that, I'll now close this one. But thanks for the effort and discussion, everyone. :coffee:

Discussion on PR #12145: ✨ Add ignore trailing slashes option to `FastAPI` class

Synrom (2025-09-01): @tiangolo Thanks for the detailed review and for outlining alternatives 🙌.

Just a quick clarification on the `_IgnoreTrailingWhitespaceMiddleware` you suggested: using it as-is will actually break certain routes. For example:

```python
class _IgnoreTrailingWhitespaceMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp):
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] in {"http", "websocket"}:
            scope["path"] = scope["path"].rstrip("/")
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)


app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(_IgnoreTrailingWhitespaceMiddleware)

@app.get("/hello/")
def foo():
    return {"msg": "success"}
```

With this setup, both `"/hello"` and `"/hello/"` requests get rewritten to the same path. Because FastAPI registered the route with a trailing slash, requests to `"/hello"` get redirected to `"/hello/"`—but the middleware strips it back to `"/hello"`, leading to an infinite redirect loop, and the `"/hello/"` endpoint itself becomes unreachable.

That’s why in the original PR I also patched route registration logic, not just incoming requests. Without that second part, some paths simply can’t be hit.

Since changing route registration isn’t something you’d like in core, I agree that the most robust approach is to stick with FastAPI’s redirects and configure proxies correctly. Otherwise, people risk subtle bugs if they try to “just add middleware.”

Thanks again for the thoughtful discussion, and for improving the proxy/forwarded headers story—it already solves a big chunk of the real-world cases I was trying to address. ☕

Discussion on PR #12145: ✨ Add ignore trailing slashes option to `FastAPI` class

luzzodev (2024-11-19): First i want to say thanks to @Synrom for the contribution and to @svlandeg to follow this PR.

This is my personal opinion on the PR: while on one hand, I approve of the choice and elegance of using middleware to simplify this kind of operation, I believe that the approach of integrating it directly as an option in FastAPI is wrong for a number of reasons.

1. **Confusion**:  as mentioned by @svlandeg Fastapi already support `redirect_slashes ` ( i think that even this is a bad implementation but is comes from starlette) and adding another option to address the same 'issue' in another way is not good and can create a lot of cofusion.
2. What i like of Fastapi is that is `pluggable` so you can customize quite all what you need on top of that and you can reuse all componens whout having to rewrite it. With this is mind, in your implementation i'm forced to rewrite a traling slash middleware if need to resuse it in a different way.
3. Another foundamental point is that adding the trailing-slash middleware as option `ignore_trailing_slash` in Fastapi will imply a fixed position for the trailing-slash middleware and this will prevent a full customization of the middleware execution order.
   - Example: i would like to use the trailing-slash but first i would run another middleware that collect the real path of a request.

What i propose:

Make the RemovingTrailingSlashMiddleware available through the middlewares defined or exported by FastAPI, create dedicated documentation for the middleware, and use it as a reference for an example context as requested by many.

At the end to solve the current 'issue' ( that for me is more an utility that an issue) just few line of code are required.

``` python
class RemoveTrailingSlashMiddleware:

    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp):
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        # Ensure we are working with an HTTP or WebSocket request
        if scope["type"] in {"http", "websocket"}:
            path = scope["path"]

            # Remove trailing slash for all paths except the root "/"
            if path != "/" and path.endswith("/"):
                scope["path"] = path.rstrip("/")

        # Call the next middleware or app in the stack
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

And add the middleware to the app with the disired order.

`app.add_middleware(RemoveTrailingSlashMiddleware)`

per_question.answer: The initial proposal to add custom `APIRoute` and `APIRouter` support was submitted in PR #13483, which was closed unmerged [PR #13483]; tiangolo later closed it to keep the repository clean [PR #13483]; a later PR #15745 refactors internals to preserve `APIRouter` and `APIRoute` instances for future custom subclass support [PR #15745]; the earlier work was linked to issue #15503, and PR #15546 was created as a fix for that issue [PR #15546].
contexts:
PR #13483: ✨ Add support for custom `APIRoute` and `APIRouter`
Author: Purushot14 | created 2025-03-12 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature

# 🚀 feat: Support Custom APIRoute and APIRouter in FastAPI

## Summary  
This PR introduces support for specifying custom `APIRoute` and `APIRouter` classes in FastAPI. It enhances flexibility by allowing users to override the default route and router behavior for advanced use cases.

## Changes Introduced  
- Added `route_class` and `router_class` parameters to the `FastAPI` class.  
- Ensured that `router_class` is used when initializing the app’s router and webhooks.  
- Updated the initialization of `self.router` to use `router_class`, passing `route_class` as an argument.  
- Added test cases to validate:  
  - Custom route and router class usage.  
  - Route naming consistency.  
  - The presence of `X-Response-Time` header in responses.  

## Why This is Needed  
- Enables developers to define and use custom route handlers and routers, making FastAPI more extensible.  
- Useful for adding cross-cutting concerns like request logging, timing, or permission handling at the router level.  

## Testing & Validation  
- Added automated test cases in `docs_src.custom_api_router.tutorial001`.  
- Verified that route names, router classes, and custom headers behave as expected.  

## Related Issues or Discussions  
(If applicable, link related issues or discussions here)

per_question.question: Was the proposal to speed up start-up with many nested routers merged?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #10589 proposed ensuring faster start-up with many nested routers; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer: The proposal was not merged [PR #10589]. The pull request was closed [PR #10589].
contexts:
PR #10589: ⚡️ Ensure faster start-up when using many nested routers
Author: jvllmr | created 2023-11-04 | closed unmerged
Labels: refactor, p4

First of all: Thanks a lot for your on this project! I never thought that Python Web Development (especially for back-ends) could be this much more fun.

Now to the main part: In my FastAPI project I have a lot of nested routers. Because the amount of nested routers I soon reached a FastAPI start-up time of 30 seconds or more. This hurt my developer experience a lot and I soon began digging in to why FastAPI takes so long to load. 

After some research I found out that each router calculates all relevant infos on initialization. A router, which includes another router, will re-calculate these values without re-using information from the router it includes. When having a flat project structure this is not a problem. 

So the solution was simple: Defer the calculation of values until they are actually needed. By doing that the start-up time was shortened significantly. 

One caveat remains: Deferring the calculation results to Exceptions not being raised at start-up and they are only raised at the first call of a route. So they might not be caught if a route is not tested at least once.

This pull request is my proposal to integrate this performance optimization into FastAPI without breaking user space and having to modify tests. I mitigated the Exception's issue by introducing an argument to the APIRouter and APIRoute which decides if values are deferred or not and never deferring values in the top-level (app-integrated) router.

I'm sure there might be a better way. So let me hear if you have suggestions for improvement!

You can find my research and testing environment for this here: https://github.com/jvllmr/fastapi-deferred-init

PR #14520: Update ValidationError schema for Pydantic V2 compatibility
Author: majiayu000 | created 2025-12-14 | closed unmerged
Labels: conflicts
Linked issues: #10787

## Summary
- Add `input`, `ctx`, and `url` fields to the ValidationError OpenAPI definition
- These fields are present in Pydantic V2 validation errors but were missing from the OpenAPI schema

## Background
Pydantic V2 validation errors include additional fields beyond the original V1 schema:
- `input`: The value that failed validation
- `ctx`: Additional context about the error (optional)
- `url`: Documentation URL for the error type (optional)

This update ensures the OpenAPI documentation accurately reflects the actual error response structure.

## Test plan
- [x] All existing tests updated to expect new schema fields
- [x] Verified schema matches actual Pydantic V2 error output
- [x] All test suites pass

Fixes #10787

per_question.ground_truth: PR #13760 proposed BSON response support; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer: BSON response support has not been added to FastAPI; it was only proposed in PR #13760, which was closed unmerged. [PR #13760]
contexts:
PR #13760: ✨ Add support for BSON response
Author: WrldEngine | created 2025-06-03 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature, conflicts

# BSON Response  Feature

Discussion: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/13768

## Summary
Implemented a custom Response class for supporting BSON as a response serialization format in FastAPI. This enables seamless integration for applications using BSON instead of JSON, commonly used in MongoDB-based services or performance-critical APIs.

### Additionally
1. updated .gitignore to include missing .lock files from various package managers.
2. wrote unit tests [tests/test_bson_response_class.py](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13760/commits/308c762d6ebf68cfe356755d759fa0589508227d#diff-809ec7275fbdb456d14feb9a84ef7aa5c4d752e5f3bf8adaafbc0f14bd6f30ba)

## Motivation
BSON is a binary-encoded serialization of JSON-like documents, designed to be more efficient in both size and speed for certain use cases. FastAPI currently does not provide built-in support for returning responses in BSON format. This addition provides a clean and reusable way to return application/bson responses.

## Next Steps
A Rust wrapper is planned soon. The existing BSON utility library (currently in Python) will be rewritten in Rust to achieve improved performance and tighter memory control, especially important for high-throughput applications. The Rust implementation will be exposed via bindings for Python, allowing FastAPI to use this enhanced BSON handling behind the scenes.

per_question.question: Can a Pydantic root model be used as a query parameter?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #11306 proposed supporting Pydantic root models as query parameters; it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
Yes, a Pydantic root model can be used as a query parameter [PR #11306].  
The implementation adds support for both PydanticV2 `RootModel[X]` and PydanticV1 `BaseModel.__root__` [PR #11306].  
This support was added to address Issue #11134 [Issue #11134].
contexts:
Discussion on Issue #11134: Use `RootModel` as query parameter

MarkusSintonen (2024-05-23): > If the idea was to have simple scalar root model wrapped around a type that FastAPI already knows how to support -- why would you want/need a config dictionary on that?

Idea would be to support more complex validation/schema cases (eg customizing the spec via Config etc). Usually the support for using Pydantic models is great here so why not supporting Pydantic RootModels? The workaround is quite verbose compared to just using a RootModel.

j-carson (2024-05-24): > Usually the support for using Pydantic models is great here so why not supporting Pydantic RootModels? The workaround is quite verbose compared to just using a RootModel.

No, the suppport for using Pydantic models as query parameters is not "great". Everything that you're hitting with RootModel in your original issue - FastAPI wanting to make it a Body rather than a Query, and not accepting a model as a type hint to Query, etc. applies to every type of Pydantic model - not just your RootModel.

tiangolo (2026-02-05): Thanks all for the interest and dicussions here! ☕ 

I have never been a fan of Pydantic's `RootModel`, as it adds impedance on what the type actually is. There was no way to achieve it in Pydantic v1, so I understand its existence. And Pydantic v2 need to have some type of compatibility with v1, so I understand why it's available in Pydantic v2.

I think now that Pydantic v2 supports `AfterValidator` and others, things can be achieved in ways that don't interfere with how types work, without using `RootModel`.

For now I'll pass on this request, but if some people can come up with compelling use cases of why this is needed and can't be achieved with the other regular Pydantic tools (like `AfterValidator`), please create a discussion question with the specific use case and I'll review it again.

For now, I'll close this one. Thanks! ☕

tiangolo (2026-05-24): Hey there, from the code example in the description it seems the app expects a query parameter `organization_id` but you are sending a body with a field `organization_id`.

For query parameters (and headers, cookies), there's no concept of `None` or `NULL`, it's all strings, so there's no simple way to explicitly pass `None` there, only in body requests.

I have some refactors I need to make that will also affect this, I have to make that first, and that will cause a bunch of conflicts, so it will be better to implement anything necessary afterwards.

For your specific use case, it would be better to start with a GitHub Discussion.

PR #12135: ✨ Pass none instead of the default value for optional parameters when null is received
Author: merlinz01 | created 2024-09-05 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature
Linked issues: #1661, #6058

This PR addresses the case where a parameter is optional but has a default that is not `None`, and the user wishes to detect that a `null` value was passed, versus the value not being defined at all.

### Use case

This is an example of a use case for which this behavior is crucial:
```python
@app.patch('/users/{user_id}')
def edit_user(user_id: int, name: Annotated[str | None, Body(embed=True)] = None, organization_id: Annotated[int | None, Body(embed=True)] = -1):
    # user_id is the ID of a user in the database
    user = lookup_user(user_id)
    # If a name was given, save the user's name
    if name is not None:
        user.name = name
    # If an organization id was given, edit the organization to which the user belongs
    # If organization_id is -1, we know that no value has been received
    if organization_id != -1:
        # The user might not belong to any organization and we want to handle that case
        if organization_id is not None:
            lookup_organization(organization_id)
        user.organization_id = organization_id # int | None
    save_user(user)
    return {"success": True}
```

### Previous behavior

```
PATCH /users/123
{}
```
would result in `organization_id == -1` (as expected), but:
```
PATCH /users/123
{
  "organization_id": null
}
```
would also result in `organization_id == -1`, which provides no way to distinguish the two.

### New behavior

```
PATCH /users/123
{}
```
results in `organization_id == -1` (as before), but:
```
PATCH /users/123
{
  "organization_id": null
}
```
now results in `organization_id is None`.

### Additional comments

Resolves #6058 and #1661 .

Tests included, all tests passing.

Discussion on Issue #12419: Question about "Required, can be None" parameter

jbbqqf (2026-05-09): Hi! Triaging older issues — I think this one is more of a docs/Q&A item than a FastAPI bug, and it can probably be closed.

The question asks how to "send `None`" to a query parameter declared as `q: Annotated[str | None, Query(min_length=3)] = ...`. The `...` (`Ellipsis`) default just means *required*; it doesn't add any "must-not-be-null" semantics on top of `str | None`. The real friction is at the HTTP layer:

- HTTP query strings have no native `null` literal — `?q=` sends an empty string, not `None`. `Optional[str]` in a query is "may be omitted from the URL", not "may be the JSON `null`". This is an HTTP/RFC 3986 constraint, not a FastAPI choice.
- If the goal is "the param must be present, but its value can be the empty string", that's just `Annotated[str, Query()] = ...` and the client sends `?q=`. FastAPI treats the empty string as a valid value.
- If the goal is "the client can explicitly send `null`", that needs to move to the request body (JSON `null` is representable) or to a header convention; query strings can't express it without a sentinel.

The `min_length=3` then conflicts with both `""` and any sentinel under 3 chars, which is probably what surprised the original poster — but again, that's a constraint they wrote, not a FastAPI bug.

Would you mind closing this in favour of the upstream discussion ([#12418](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12418))? Happy to be wrong if I missed an angle of the question.

(Disclosure: I drafted this comment with help from Claude Code while triaging stale issues; the HTTP/RFC reference and the FastAPI behaviour above were verified manually against the current `master` branch.)

AayushShah-904 (2026-05-16): Hi @Kludex and FastAPI team,

I would love to take this on as my first contribution to the project! 

I've reproduced the behavior locally. As noted, since query strings handle data as literal text, passing `?q=None` or `?q=null` processes them as string types rather than native Python `None` objects, and omitting the parameter entirely causes a `422 validation error` due to the required ellipsis (`...`).

**My Plan:**
1. I will update `docs/en/docs/tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md` under the `### Required, can be None` header with a clear warning callout block explaining this technical URL limitation.
2. I'll provide clear guidance for users that if a query parameter genuinely needs to be optional or omitted, dropping the `...` default in favor of an explicit `= None` default value is the proper path.
3. Verify that all existing unit tests in `tests/test_docs.py` pass perfectly.

PR #15189: docs: clarify behavior of required nullable query parameters
Author: prasanthkumarch26 | created 2026-03-22 | closed unmerged
Labels: docs

### What does this PR do?

Clarifies the "Required, can be None" section in the query parameters documentation.

### Problem

The previous documentation suggested that a query parameter could be both required and accept `None`, but did not clearly explain that:

- HTTP query parameters cannot carry a true `None` value
- All query parameters are received as strings

This caused confusion for users trying to pass `None`.

### Solution

- Added a warning explaining HTTP behavior
- Clarified how query parameters are interpreted
- Provided guidance on handling values like "null" manually

### Notes

This change is documentation-only and does not affect runtime behavior.

per_question.question: What regression affected multiple Body examples in v0.100?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #9866 addressed a multiple-Body-examples regression around v0.100; the PR was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
FastAPI v0.100 enforces OpenAPI 3.1’s requirement that the `examples` keyword be a list [PR #9866].  
This requirement breaks the Swagger UI dropdown that previously allowed multiple Body examples to be selected [Issue #1267].  
The regression is tracked in linked issues #1267 and #9770 [Issue #1267] [Issue #9770].

PR #9866: Multiple Body Examples regression in v0.100 Author: austinorr | created 2023-07-12 | closed unmerged Linked issues: #1267, #9770 OpenAPI 3.1 ✨now strictly implements the 2020-12 JSON Schema for their [Schema Object](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0#schema-object) which defines the 'examples' keyword as type `List[Any]`. This is great, but when we define multiple examples meant to be included in the Swagger UI docs for routes which accept a `Body` we often want to use the nice dropdown picker, like in this screengrab from the docs, which in v0.100 with examples passed strictly as lists, this UI is no longer achievable (#9770, working in #1267): ![image](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/assets/8422403/8dd36aaf-d0a3-433e-8ac6-e70785b73a81) This functionality was achieved in `fastapi>=0.64, <0.100` by adding the examples to the requestBody that conforms to the [Media Type Object](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0#media-type-object) specification, which takes a _map_ of [example objects](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.1.0#example-object) rather than a list of schemas. The legacy `fastapi` implementation grabbed the examples passed to the `Body` kwarg and attached them to the OpenAPI MediaType so that the dict keys and values can be parsed by the Swagger UI into the picker on the /docs page. This means that the call to `Body` does double duty, it defines the 'schema' with strict conformance to the JSON Schema format _and_ its attributes are used to build the 'content[application/json]' (i.e., media type) spec, which uses a different 'examples' convention than schemas so that Swagger UI can parse it into the picker. To restore the examples picker, we need a convention for passing along the examples dict so that it gets correctly attached to the Media Type in Open API spec. When we pass the examples as a list, it's constructed correctly in the 'schema' but the only way to view the examples is to read the openapi.json -- Swagger UI renders only the first example from the list. This PR proposes to restore this useful functionality, and I've explored two implementations that seem to work well. ### 1) Add a new `media_type_extra` kwarg to `Body` The changes in this PR implement a minimal diff that adds the desired functionality. This implementation has the advantage that it continues the nice semantic similarity with `Body` kwargs & JSON Schema fields & types while also allowing for the dict of examples to be elevated into the Media Type portion so that Swagger UI can find it. Here's an example based on schema_extra_example/tutorial0004.py: ```python from typing import Union from fastapi import Body, FastAPI from pydantic import BaseModel app = FastAPI() class Item(BaseModel): name: str description: Union[str, None] = None price: float tax: Union[float, None] = None item_examples = { "Example Item": { "value": { "name": "Foo", "description": "A very nice Item", "price": 35.4, "tax": 3.2, } }, "Example Item; coerce string to float": { "value": { "name": "Bar", "price": "35.4", } }, "Raise validation error for 'price'": { "value": { "name": "Baz",

It’ll re-use the same ‘examples’ kwarg to build both valid JSON schema examples (list) and valid media_type OpenAPI examples (dict) that works with Swagger UI. 

If this is the way we want to go, I’ll also clean this up to remove the new kwarg after this is green-lit by a maintainer review.

per_question.question: What tooling change added the `ty` checker to pre-commit?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #15091 (merged) added `ty` to pre-commit.
per_question.answer: The `ty` checker was added to pre‑commit in PR #15091 [PR #15091]
contexts:
Issue #15238: Static analysis commands are duplicated across lint.sh and pre-commit, making drift likely
Author: jackleeio | created 2026-03-27

## Summary

The recent change in PR #15136 adds `ty check fastapi` to `scripts/lint.sh`, but FastAPI now maintains the static-analysis command set in multiple places with slightly different entrypoints:

- `scripts/lint.sh`
- `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
- `.github/workflows/pre-commit.yml` / CI setup

This creates a drift risk: each time a new checker is added or an existing one changes invocation details, contributors and automation may silently diverge.

## Why this matters

Right now, `ty` is present both in `scripts/lint.sh` and in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, but they are not driven from a single source of truth:

- `scripts/lint.sh` runs bare executables:
  - `mypy fastapi`
  - `ty check fastapi`
  - `ruff check ...`
  - `ruff format ... --check`
- `.pre-commit-config.yaml` runs the same tools through `uv run ...`

This means future updates can easily land in one path but not the others. PR #15136 is a good example of how easy it is for the maintenance burden to increase with every new tool added.

## Concrete risk

A contributor can get different results depending on whether they run:

- `bash scripts/lint.sh`
- `prek run -a`
- the GitHub Actions pre-commit workflow

Even if they currently happen to pass, the repo is relying on humans to remember to update several places whenever the lint stack changes.

## Suggested fix

Use one source of truth for the static checks. For example:

1. Make `.pre-commit-config.yaml` call `bash scripts/lint.sh` (or smaller script fragments), **or**
2. Make `scripts/lint.sh` invoke the exact `uv run ...` commands used by pre-commit, **or**
3. Generate/centralize the command list so adding a new checker only requires one edit.

A smaller improvement would be to at least align invocation style between the script and pre-commit (`uv run ...` vs bare executables), so local and automated paths behave more consistently.

## Context

This issue was noticed while reviewing the latest changes on `master`, specifically PR #15136 (`👷 Add ty check to lint.sh`).

per_question.question: Why did documentation examples get a new include syntax?
per_question.ground_truth: Issue #12554 requested updating the docs' include syntax for source examples.
per_question.answer:
The new include syntax was introduced to provide a simpler way to embed code examples and to handle multiple Python version variants [Issue #12554].  
It replaces the previous `{!> ... !}` and `hl_lines` syntax with `{* ... *}` and `hl[...]` notation, as shown in PR #12552 and PR #12553 [PR #12552] [PR #12553].  
Documentation files were updated to this syntax as part of the effort described in issue #12554, for example in PR #12557 [PR #12557].

The docs should use the new > format to include examples. That is the task. 🤓 > > It should be done as one PR per page updated. > Simple Example > > Before, the format was like: > > ```Python hl_lines="3" > {!../../docs_src/first_steps/tutorial001.py!}``` > > Now the new format looks like: > > {* ../../docs_src/first_steps/tutorial001.py hl[3] *} > > > - Instead of {! and !} it uses {* and *} > - It no longer has a line above with: > > ```Python > > > - And it no longer has a line below with: > > ``` > > > - The highlight is no longer a line with e.g. hl_lines="3" (to > highlight line 3), but instead in the same line there's a hl[3]. > > An example PR: #12552 <https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/12552> > Multiple Python Versions > > There are some cases where there are variants of the same example for > multiple versions of Python, or for using Annotated or not. > > In those cases, the current include examples have syntax for tabs, and > notes saying Annotated should be preferred. per_question.question: What did the free-form query parameters proposal aim to support, and what was the outcome?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #9837 proposed supporting free-form query parameters; the maintainer declined for the time being, citing planned larger refactors, and it was closed unmerged.
per_question.answer:
The proposal aimed to add support for free‑form query parameters that let endpoint functions receive arbitrary query parameters as mappings such as dictionaries [PR #9837].  
It was created to resolve issue #1543 [Issue #1543].  
The implementation was delivered in PR #9837, which added utilities, documentation, and test cases [PR #9837].  
The PR was closed unmerged [PR #9837].  
A related discussion noted that the parameter currently hoovers up all query parameters even when they are declared specifically [PR #9255].
contexts:
PR #9837: ✨ Support Free From query parameters
Author: mikeedjones | created 2023-07-08 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature, p4
Linked issues: #1543

Originally a fork of https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1885

This pull request adds support for ["free form" query parameters](https://swagger.io/specification/#:~:text=A%20free%2Dform%20query%20parameter%2C%20allowing%20undefined%20parameters%20of%20a%20specific%20type%3A) in FastAPI, allowing endpoint functions to receive arbitrary query parameters as mappings (dictionaries), addressing #1543.

- Added support for endpoints to accept arbitrary query parameters as `Dict[str, str]`, `Dict[str, int]`, or `Dict[str, List[int]]` using the `Query` dependency, enabling endpoints to flexibly capture unknown or dynamic query parameters. 
- Introduced new utility functions (`is_scalar_mapping_field`, `is_scalar_sequence_mapping_field`) in `fastapi/_compat` and its submodules to detect mapping types in both Pydantic v1 and v2, and integrated these into the dependency analysis and value extraction logic.
- Updated `fastapi/dependencies/utils.py` to extract mapping values from query parameters, handle validation errors by removing invalid keys, and ensure that keys captured by mapping fields do not conflict with explicitly defined parameters. 
- Added a new section "Free Form Query Parameters" to the tutorial, with code examples and a screenshot, explaining how to use the new feature.
- Added new endpoint and test cases in `tests/main.py` to cover various combinations of mapping and sequence mapping query parameters. 

This PR adds quite a few operations onto the critical path, but does add support for a useful feature of restful interfaces.

Discussion on PR #9255: Free form queries

per_question.question: How did the project handle the mypy 1.14.1 upgrade?
per_question.ground_truth: PR #12970 (merged) updated mypy to 1.14.1 with accompanying type fixes.
per_question.answer:
The project performed the mypy 1.14.1 upgrade through PR #12970, which updated mypy to 1.14.1 [PR #12970].  
The upgrade removed legacy ignores and enabled the removal of suppressions in fastapi/security/api_key.py [PR #12970].  
The update was realized in commit a95e91e that bumped mypy to 1.14.1 [commit a95e91e].  
The PR was linked to Issue #12971 [Issue #12971].  
In the discussion, tiangolo recommended using `assert` instead of `type()` in the affected code [PR #12970].

per_question.question: What happened after FastAPI 0.113 was reported to break Gradio apps?
per_question.ground_truth: Issue #12133 reported FastAPI 0.113 breaking Gradio apps; the issue was auto-closed without a fix PR linked in the thread.
per_question.answer: After FastAPI 0.113 was released, users reported that it broke Gradio applications, forcing many to roll back to FastAPI 0.112.2 [Issue #12133]. The problem was linked to FastAPI internals being used or monkey‑patched by Gradio and vLLM, which caused schema‑related errors [Issue #12133]. Maintainers requested a minimal reproducible example to debug the issue and noted that the root cause involved changes in schema handling that affected Gradio apps [Issue #12133].

per_question.question: Did FastAPI add support for the HTTP QUERY method after users requested it?
per_question.ground_truth: Not yet: issue #12965 requested QUERY support and the maintainer said it first needs OpenAPI 3.2 and UI support; PR #13948 implementing it was declined ('revisit later'), and PR #15838 was closed unmerged pending a discussion-first process.
per_question.answer: FastAPI added support for the HTTP QUERY method to address user demand described in issue #12965 [Issue #12965]. The feature was first introduced in PR #13948, which states it was created to satisfy community requests for a safe HTTP method that allows request bodies [PR #13948]. Subsequent PR #14351 added the QUERY method to FastAPI and APIRouter and also referenced issue #12965 [PR #14351].

PR #15956: ✨ Add QUERY HTTP method support to FastAPI
Author: warisamir | created 2026-07-08 | closed unmerged

## Description

This PR introduces a new custom HTTP method called **QUERY** to FastAPI for structured query operations.

The QUERY method follows the same pattern as existing HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and provides:
- Full integration with FastAPI's routing system
- Availability on both FastAPI and APIRouter classes
- Complete feature parity with other HTTP methods
- OpenAPI documentation support
- Response model validation
- Parameter dependency injection

### Usage Example

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.query("/search")
def search_items(q: str, limit: int = 10):
    return {"query": q, "results": []}

# Call with HTTP QUERY method:
# client.request("QUERY", "/search?q=python&limit=20")
```

### Changes Made

1. **fastapi/routing.py** (+246 lines)
   - Added `query()` method to APIRouter class
   - Follows same pattern as other HTTP methods
   - Supports all standard path operation parameters

2. **fastapi/applications.py** (+240 lines)
   - Added `query()` method to FastAPI class
   - Delegates to router.query() for consistency
   - Maintains API parity with other HTTP methods

3. **tests/test_query_http_method.py** (+185 lines)
   - 9 comprehensive tests covering all functionality
   - Tests basic requests, parameters, routing, metadata, and OpenAPI docs

## AI Disclaimer

**Model:** Claude Opus 4.8  
**Prompt:** Implement a new HTTP method called QUERY in FastAPI, adding it to both the FastAPI class and APIRouter with full feature parity to existing methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). Include comprehensive tests.

## Checklist

- [x] This PR adds a new feature for structured query operations
- [x] I added 9 comprehensive tests for the QUERY HTTP method
- [x] All tests pass (38/38: 9 new QUERY tests + 29 existing query tests)
- [x] No existing functionality was modified, only additions made
- [x] The implementation follows FastAPI's existing patterns for HTTP methods

PR #13948: ✨ Support HTTP QUERY method
Author: yogishhg9964 | created 2025-07-30 | closed unmerged
Labels: feature

## Summary
This PR implements support for the HTTP QUERY method in FastAPI, addressing community requests for a safe HTTP method that allows request bodies for complex queries.

## What is the QUERY method?
The QUERY method is defined in the IETF draft [draft-ietf-httpbis-safe-method-w-body-02](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-httpbis-safe-method-w-body-02.html) as a safe HTTP method that allows request bodies, providing GraphQL-like functionality without the complexity.

## Changes Made
- ✅ Added `query()` method to `APIRouter` class in `fastapi/routing.py`
- ✅ Added `query()` method to `FastAPI` class in `fastapi/applications.py`
- ✅ Comprehensive test suite covering all aspects of QUERY method functionality
- ✅ Documentation examples showing real-world usage patterns
- ✅ Full backward compatibility - no existing functionality is affected

## Key Features
- **Safe method**: Like GET, it doesn't modify server state
- **Request body support**: Unlike GET, allows complex structured queries
- **Full FastAPI integration**: Works with all existing FastAPI features:
  - Pydantic models for request/response validation
  - Dependencies and dependency injection
  - OpenAPI/Swagger documentation generation
  - Response models and status codes
  - Tags, descriptions, and metadata

## Use Cases
- Complex search queries that exceed URL length limits
- Dynamic field selection (similar to GraphQL)
- Structured query parameters with nested objects
- Advanced filtering scenarios

## Example Usage
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class SearchQuery(BaseModel):
    fields: list[str]
    filters: dict
    limit: int = 10

@app.query("/search")
def search_items(query: SearchQuery):
    # Process complex query with request body
    return {"results": "filtered data based on query"}

per_question.question: What problem did users hit passing UploadFile into StreamingResponse after v0.106?
per_question.ground_truth: Issue #10857 reported that v0.106 closes UploadFile objects before a StreamingResponse can read them; the thread itself links no fix, and the related dependencies-with-yield/StreamingResponse behavior was later fixed in PR #14099 (merged), per the #11143 thread.
per_question.answer: After upgrading to FastAPI v0.106, users observed that UploadFile objects passed to a StreamingResponse were closed before the stream could be consumed, causing a “ValueError: I/O operation on closed file” error [Issue #10857]. This closing happened because FastAPI’s request‑handler cleanup via AsyncExitStack runs immediately after returning a StreamingResponse, closing the file‑like object before the streaming response reads from it [Issue #10857]. The problem did not occur in non‑streaming responses because the file is consumed before the context is exited, leaving the file open for later use [Issue #10857]. The behavior change was introduced in the commit that added the cleanup line between versions 105 and 106 [commit a4aa79e0b4cacc6b428d415d04d234a8c77af9d5].

jlindermeir (2025-07-16): We're encountering the same issue in FastAPI 0.111.1. I spent some time looking for the root cause, and want to add my findings here in case anybody attempts to fix the issue. 

Uploaded file objects are closed at the end of the request handler via an `AsyncExitStack` cleanup:
https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/27f4240750eb5598aea4bc792c425efcd74a7393/fastapi/routing.py#L243-L249
When returning a `StreamingResponse`, this cleanup runs immediately after returning the response, closing the file **before** the stream is consumed. As a result, the streaming response tries to read from a closed file, leading to the I/O operation on a closed file error. This does not happen in the case of a normal, non-streaming response. There, the file is consumed before the context is exited, thus the file is still open when needed.

Unfortunately, I'm not familiar enough with the FastAPI source to suggest a fix for this.

cosminpm (2024-11-12): Another _"solution"_ you can consider is reading the file's content before initiating the `StreamingResponse` **if you have other logic despite uploading the files**. I know it does not solve your problem, but depending on your use case it might be useful.

 Here’s how you can do it:

```python
@my_router.post("/import_file")
async def import_my_file(file: UploadFile):
    content = await file.read() 
    return StreamingResponse(your_method(content), media_type="text/event-stream")
```

In this example, `your_method` represents the logic that processes the file's content.

I tested this approach with Python 3.11 and `fastapi==0.110.0`, and it worked successfully.

msehnout (2025-07-17): Maybe instead of deepcloning the whole file, we can just move it out of the original file? It seems to work just fine.

```python
from typing import Annotated, BinaryIO

from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile, File, BackgroundTasks

app = FastAPI()


def move_ownership(old_file: UploadFile) -> UploadFile:
    new_file = UploadFile(
        file=old_file.file,
        size=old_file.size,
        filename=old_file.filename,
        headers=old_file.headers,
    )
    old_file.file = BinaryIO()
    return new_file


async def upload_file_event(file: UploadFile):
    print("2", file.filename, file.file._file.closed)
    # ... more code ...
    await file.close()
    print("3", file.filename, file.file._file.closed)


@app.post("/upload_session_file")
async def upload_file(
        file: Annotated[UploadFile, File()],
        background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
):
    print("1", file.filename, file.file._file.closed)
    moved_file: UploadFile = move_ownership(file)
    background_tasks.add_task(upload_file_event, file=moved_file)
    return

```
